name: World War I
description: The Great War, 1914–1918 — battles, leaders, and weapons

questions.q: In which year did World War I begin?

source.notes: Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia July 28, 1914.

questions.q: In which year did World War I officially end?

source.notes: The armistice took effect at 11 a.m. on November 11, 1918.

questions.q: On which continent was most of World War I fought?

source.notes: The Western and Eastern Fronts stretched across Europe; lesser campaigns were fought in Africa and the Middle East.

questions.q: In which Bosnian city was Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated, triggering World War I?

source.notes: Assassinated June 28, 1914 while visiting Sarajevo in Austria-Hungary-controlled Bosnia.

questions.q: Which red flower is worn in many countries every November as a symbol of remembrance for World War I dead?

source.notes: Inspired by John McCrae's 1915 poem 'In Flanders Fields'.

questions.q: What was the name of the main peace treaty signed in 1919 that ended the state of war between Germany and the Allies?

source.notes: Signed in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles on June 28, 1919.

questions.q: The sinking of which British ocean liner in 1915 outraged the United States and pushed it toward entering the war?

source.notes: Torpedoed by the German U-boat U-20 on May 7, 1915; 1,198 people died.

questions.q: In which year did the United States officially enter World War I?

source.notes: Congress declared war on Germany April 6, 1917.

questions.q: What was the common slang nickname for British soldiers during World War I?

source.notes: Short for 'Tommy Atkins', a generic British rifleman name on sample Army forms.

questions.q: What were Germany, Austria-Hungary, and the Ottoman Empire collectively known as?

source.notes: Bulgaria joined them in 1915.

questions.q: What post-war international organization was founded in 1920 to prevent another world war?

source.notes: Proposed in Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points; the U.S. never joined.

questions.q: What was the nickname of the red-painted-aircraft-flying German fighter ace Manfred von Richthofen?

source.notes: Credited with 80 air-combat victories before being killed in April 1918.

questions.q: Who assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand?

source.notes: 19-year-old Bosnian Serb nationalist affiliated with the Black Hand secret society.

questions.q: Who was the German Kaiser throughout World War I?

source.notes: Abdicated November 9, 1918 and went into exile in the Netherlands.

questions.q: Who became British Prime Minister in December 1916 and led the country to the end of the war?

source.notes: Replaced Asquith in December 1916; served until 1922.

questions.q: What does 'ANZAC' stand for?
choices: Australia and New Zealand Army Corps

source.notes: Formed in Egypt in 1915; remembered on ANZAC Day, April 25.

questions.q: During the 1914 Christmas Truce on the Western Front, what sport did some British and German troops reportedly play together in no-man's-land?

source.notes: Ad hoc matches are documented in several unit diaries.

questions.q: Which month of 1918 saw the armistice end the fighting on the Western Front?

source.notes: 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month, 1918.

questions.q: What was the name of Germany's pre-war strategy to knock France out quickly before turning east to fight Russia?

source.notes: Drafted by Count Alfred von Schlieffen in 1905; failed at the First Battle of the Marne.

questions.q: In April 1915 Allied troops — especially ANZACs — landed on which Ottoman peninsula in a costly campaign?

source.notes: Campaign ended in Allied evacuation January 1916; over 100,000 died.

questions.q: The 1918–1920 global pandemic that killed more people than the war itself is commonly known as what?

source.notes: Estimated 50 million dead worldwide; named not for its origin but because neutral Spain reported freely.

questions.q: The Dutch-born exotic dancer Margaretha Zelle was executed by France in 1917 for espionage. By what stage name was she known?

source.notes: Executed by firing squad October 15, 1917, outside Paris.
